claim_boundary.what_is_not_supported: This is still a finite computational-basis action model, not a complex linear operator, Hamiltonian sum, Hermiticity proof, spectral theorem, Quantum PCP/NLTS theorem, global impossibility result, BQP separation, or quantum-advantage claim.
claim_boundary.what_is_supported: Lean checks that two Pauli terms with disjoint site supports commute under the restricted computational-basis replay, including final basis state and accumulated phase.

command: mkdir -p .lake/build/lib/B9/ClusterStabilizer && lake env lean -o .lake/build/lib/B9/ClusterStabilizer/WidthLocality.olean B9/ClusterStabilizer/WidthLocality.lean && lake env lean -o .lake/build/lib/B9/ClusterStabilizer/PauliBasisAction.olean B9/ClusterStabilizer/PauliBasisAction.lean && lake env lean -o .lake/build/lib/B9/ClusterStabilizer/PauliActionComposition.olean B9/ClusterStabilizer/PauliActionComposition.lean && lake env lean -o .lake/build/lib/B9/ClusterStabilizer/PauliActionCommutation.olean B9/ClusterStabilizer/PauliActionCommutation.lean && lake env lean B9/ClusterStabilizer/PauliActionTermCommutation.lean

requirements.label: Lean and Lake version probes return zero
All five B9 modules compile together
The source defines a factor-to-term disjointness predicate
A factor commutes with an entire disjoint term
The theorem reuses the prior factor-level commutation proof
The theorem reuses compositional replay associativity
The source proves whole-term disjoint replay commutation
The term theorem quantifies over both site supports
The checked module contains no matrix or complex-amplitude machinery
Fresh transcript binds every source and project hash
The proof is finite replay semantics rather than a spectral theorem
The source does not claim BQP or Quantum PCP resolution
